era_context: Identical to -2080: Old Kingdom Egypt fragmenting, Akkadian Empire collapsing under Gutian pressure, Indus Valley Civilization in mature urban phase, Xia Dynasty (if historical) in early phase in China.

events.title: Continued Akkadian fragmentation; Gutian consolidation in Mesopotamia
events.description: The Akkadian state continues its dissolution. Gutian warbands, now more organized, consolidate control over central and southern Mesopotamia. Sumerian city-states in the south (Lagash, Uruk, Nippur) reassert local autonomy but remain vulnerable to Gutian raids. This year falls within the broader period of Akkadian collapse (c. 2200–2100 BCE), with no specific dated events surviving in the historical record.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are dated to this year in surviving sources. This entry describes the broader context of the Akkadian collapse, which occurred over several decades. The year is assigned by convention within the collapse period.

events.title: Egyptian Old Kingdom: continued regional autonomy of nomarchs
events.description: The fragmentation of Egyptian central authority continues. Regional nomarchs maintain and expand their autonomous power bases. No specific events are recorded for this year, but the broader trend of decentralization continues unabated. The pharaonic center at Memphis loses further control over provincial resources and military forces.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from provincial administrative centers

events.certainty_note: No specific events are dated to this year. This entry describes the ongoing process of Old Kingdom fragmentation.
cross_references: Part of Old Kingdom decline (c. -2200 to -2055)

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, it should be noted that no specific historical events are attested for this particular year in any surviving source. The entries above describe broader processes rather than discrete events.
historiographic_note: This year has virtually no specific documentation. The entries above are inferences from broader archaeological and textual patterns. The historical record for this year is essentially blank; what is presented is context rather than history. This illustrates the severe documentation gaps for the early second millennium BCE outside of a few major civilizations.
